briefs.situation_overview: Iran remains under comprehensive Western sanctions following the collapse of the JCPOA revival talks. The government faces dual pressure from domestic economic strain and external nuclear negotiations.
briefs.key_dynamics: The Islamic Republic balances factional politics between hardliners and pragmatists while managing an economy hit by oil export restrictions and dollar-denominated sanctions.
briefs.historical_roots: Iran's revolutionary identity, shaped by the 1979 Islamic Revolution and the 1980-88 Iran-Iraq War, drives its anti-Western posture. The 2015 JCPOA and its 2018 US withdrawal under Trump created the current nuclear impasse.
briefs.actor_map: Supreme Leader Khamenei holds ultimate authority. IRGC controls key economic sectors and proxy networks. President Pezeshkian represents pragmatist faction. Hardliners dominate parliament.

alignment_map.primary_alignment: Russia-China axis — deepening defense and trade ties as Western isolation increases
alignment_map.secondary_ties: Maintains outreach to Global South; Qatar serves as informal channel to US
alignment_map.internal_factions: Pragmatists favor diplomatic engagement; IRGC and hardliners prefer resistance economy
alignment_map.fault_lines: Economic deterioration could strengthen reformist voices; nuclear escalation risks IRGC dominance

watchlist: IAEA quarterly report on uranium enrichment levels
IRGC proxy activity in Iraq and Syria as tension indicator
IRR exchange rate as economic pressure gauge
